topic: Marin Daily: Component sourcing, optical stack, electrical design, firmware progress — Saturday Feb 28
summary: Advanced optical system approved with dual-supplier risk mitigation strategy and parallel technical development.

participants.name: Theo
participants.description: Strategic project manager for Marin. Coordinates all workstreams, tracks timeline, manages dependencies, and ensures the Day 30 gate is met.

participants.name: Joshua Levy
participants.description: CTO of Marin. Leads product research and development. Owns technical architecture, component selection, and engineering direction.

participants.name: Joe
participants.description: CEO of Marin. Owns company vision, marketing strategy, and company direction. Makes final business decisions.

participants.name: Palmer Luckey
participants.description: Optical engineer for Marin. Founded Oculus VR at 19, designed the Rift optics, founded Anduril Industries. Expert in display optics, Fresnel/TIR lenses, diffusion systems, and perceptual uniformity. Designs the optical stack: LED collimation, diffusion layers, reflective control, and seam blending.

participants.name: Nikola Tesla
participants.description: Electrical engineer for Marin. Designs power system (12-24V DC), STM32H7 control board, LED driver circuits (TLC5940/5971), Ethernet interface, thermal management, and PCB architecture.

participants.name: John Carmack
participants.description: Firmware and software engineer for Marin. Writes STM32H7 firmware (engine addressing, PWM, gamma, thermal), host API (REST/WebSocket), animation engine, OTA updates, and diagnostics.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. Let's dive into the Marin project status. Based on our last conversation about the Mosaic build pipeline, I want to focus today on component sourcing progress, optical stack design decisions, electrical system architecture, and firmware development. Palmer, let's start with you - where do we stand on the optical components and what's blocking us?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: The optical stack is coming together, but we're hitting some supply chain friction. I've been working on the LED collimation system and diffusion layers for seamless blending. The challenge is finding the right combination of Fresnel lenses and TIR optics that can handle the brightness uniformity we need across large display surfaces. I've identified three potential suppliers for the custom diffusion films, but lead times are 8-12 weeks. That's cutting it close for our Day 30 gate.

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: Palmer, what are the power requirements for your LED array? I'm designing the driver circuits using TLC5940 chips, but I need to know the current draw per segment to size the power system properly. Are we talking 12V or 24V DC input, and how many channels do we need to drive independently?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Each LED segment needs about 2 amps at 12V for full brightness. We're looking at roughly 200 individual zones for proper uniformity control. The real challenge is thermal - at full power, we're dissipating significant heat that could affect the optical properties of the diffusion layers.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: That thermal constraint is going to impact my PWM control algorithms. I'm building the engine addressing system on the STM32H7, but if we have to throttle brightness based on temperature, I need real-time thermal feedback loops built into the firmware. How precise does the zone-by-zone dimming need to be?

transcript.speaker: Joshua Levy
transcript.message: Hold on - before we get deeper into technical specs, what's our component sourcing timeline looking like? Palmer mentioned 8-12 week lead times. That puts us at risk for the Day 30 milestone. Do we have backup suppliers identified?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: I've got three quotes, but honestly, two of the suppliers are just resellers of the same manufacturer in Shenzhen. The third option is a US-based company with faster turnaround but 40% higher cost. We might need to bite the bullet on pricing to hit our timeline.

transcript.speaker: Joe
transcript.message: What's the cost impact? And more importantly, what's the market differentiation we get from this optical approach versus simpler alternatives?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: The seamless blending system gives us uniform brightness across joints that competitors can't match. It's the difference between a professional display and something that looks like tiled monitors. But we're talking about $15K in optical components versus $8K for a basic approach.

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: The electrical complexity scales with Palmer's design too. More zones means more driver chips, more complex PCB routing, more thermal management. I can make it work, but it adds 3-4 weeks to my board design and testing cycle.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: And firmware complexity grows exponentially. Individual zone control, gamma correction per segment, thermal compensation - we're looking at a much more sophisticated control system. I can build it, but it needs more memory and processing power than I initially spec'd.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: So we have a classic time-cost-quality triangle here. Joshua, as CTO, what's your call? Do we go premium with the advanced optical system and accept the timeline and cost impact, or do we simplify for the Day 30 gate?

transcript.speaker: Joshua Levy
transcript.message: Palmer, can you give me a honest assessment - is the optical uniformity a must-have for our target market, or a nice-to-have? I need to understand if this is what wins deals or just impresses engineers.

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: It's what separates us from cheap LED walls. Anyone can tile displays together. Our value prop is professional-grade visual quality. If we ship something that looks like a patchwork of screens, we're competing on price instead of quality. That's not a fight we want to be in.

transcript.speaker: Joe
transcript.message: Palmer's right about differentiation. But Theo, what happens to our other milestones if we commit to the complex optical path? Are we robbing from other critical workstreams?

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: I can parallelize some of my PCB work while waiting for Palmer's final optical specs. The power system design is mostly independent. But I need firm requirements on power budget and thermal constraints within the next week to keep the electrical design on track.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: Same here - I can build the basic STM32H7 architecture and communication systems now, but the advanced zone control algorithms need Palmer's optical model to be locked down. Give me the API specification and I can develop against it.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Alright, here's what I'm hearing. We have interdependent technical decisions that need to be made quickly to avoid blocking downstream work. Palmer, you need to make the supplier decision this week. Nikola, you need power and thermal specs. John, you need the control API defined. Joshua, we need your technical architecture decision - advanced optical system or simplified approach?

transcript.speaker: Joshua Levy
transcript.message: Let's go with the advanced system, but with risk mitigation. Palmer, place orders with both the premium US supplier and the backup option. We'll eat the deposit cost to derisk our timeline. Nikola, design for the high-power scenario but keep a simplified fallback option. John, same approach - build the complex control system but keep a basic mode as backup.

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: That works for me. I'll have supplier contracts signed by Monday and optical component specs locked by Wednesday. That gives Nikola and John what they need to proceed in parallel.

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: Perfect. I'll finalize the power system design assuming worst-case thermal load and have PCB layouts ready for review by end of week. The modular approach lets us scale back if needed.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: I can work with that timeline. I'll have the basic communication and control framework running by next week, with the advanced zone control algorithms following once Palmer's specs are locked.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Excellent. So our immediate priorities are: Palmer locks supplier contracts and specs by Wednesday, Nikola delivers power system design by Friday, John has basic firmware framework running by Friday. Joshua, we'll need final architecture approval after Palmer's specs are locked. We're de-risking the Day 30 gate while maintaining our technical differentiation. Any final concerns before we close?

transcript.speaker: Joe
transcript.message: Just one - make sure we're documenting these technical decisions and their business rationale. This optical approach better deliver the market differentiation we're betting on. Palmer, I want to see competitive analysis showing why our approach wins deals.

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Absolutely. I'll include market positioning analysis with the technical specs. The visual quality difference should be immediately obvious in side-by-side demos.

actionItems.text: Lock optical component supplier contracts and technical specifications
Design high-power electrical system with modular fallback options
Develop basic STM32H7 firmware framework with advanced control capability
Approve final technical architecture after optical specs locked
Complete competitive analysis for optical system market positioning

topPriorities: Lock optical component suppliers and specs by Wednesday
Complete power system design for high-performance configuration
Develop modular firmware architecture with basic framework operational

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
